name: Book Sale and 'Poster Women' Exhibition

location.name: Goethe-Institut / Max Mueller Bhavan New  New

description: Saturday, 18 and Sunday, 19 April 2026, 11:00 to 19:00To markWomen’s History MonthandDalit History Month,Zubaan Publishersis delighted to invite you to aMega Book Salefeaturing all available Zubaan titles—fiction, non-fiction, and academic—at discounted prices. Whether you are an avid reader or looking to gift meaningful books and merchandise to your loved ones, we have something for everyone.In addition to theBook Sale, we’re thrilled to present thePoster Women Exhibition, a curated showcase that dives deep into the visual history of the women’s movement in India. Since its inception in 2006,Poster Womenhas been a striking project that maps this incredible journey through captivating posters that tell the stories of activism, resilience, and transformation.For those seeking imaginative stories for younger readers, ourYoung Zubaancollection has you covered! We feature inspiring literature tailored for ages 4 to 16, perfect for sparking curiosity and joy in young minds.And don’t miss ourWarehouse Wonderssection, where select titles are available at a thrilling 50% discount!Plus, here’s the icing on the cake: meet some of our talented authors, engage with them, and get your books signed!Join us and spend a few hours immersed in powerful narratives that rekindle hope and celebrate feminist solidarities.Come browse, discover, and take home stories that will challenge, comfort, and inspire. We can’t wait to see you there!Panel on Saturday, 18 April 202618:00 onwardsThe Books We MadeGenerally, in publishing, editors commission writers to write books and then those books are published. But Zubaan’s books are a bit different: most of them grow out of the women’s movement and the felt need, or articulation, of issues on the ground. For readers, these stories of how certain books came about are often as, if not more, interesting than the books themselves. In this panel we explore three Zubaan books, one made by 75 village women in Rajasthan, one by a writer with a long involvement in Kashmir and the third, a book on street theatre in India. Come and listen to stories of how writers and activists created their books.Speakers:Manisha Chaudhry, currently head of Jaipur Bookmark, previously head of publishing at Pratham books, translator, editor (in English and Hindi) and expert on children’s books. Manisha studied at JNU and Jamia universities in Delhi, she worked in Kali for Women, Zubaan’s predecessor and has been active in the women’s movement.Sahba Husainis an independent researcher and women’s rights activist. She has travelled extensively in Jammu and Kashmir, documenting the social consequences of armed conflict, mass violence and militarization on individuals, families and communities, with a special focus on women. Her earlier research focused on problems of working women in the organized and unorganized sectors in several states of India and on the impact of development policies on women’s social, political and economic status.Deepti Priya Mehrotrais a political scientist with trans-disciplinary interests. She has engaged with street theatre and the women’s movement since the late 1970s. She writes in English and Hindi, and has taught political science, sociology, gender studies and media studies, variously at Delhi University, TISS-Mumbai, and other places.Currently, she is working on a photo archive of the autonomous women’s movement, teaching a course in educational philosophy, and collaborating with social organisations in activist, research and advisory roles.Moderator:Urvashi Butaliaco-founded Kali for Women in 1984 and, in 2003, Zubaan. With over 50 years of experience in feminist and independent publishing. She also has a long involvement in the women’s movement in India, and is a well-known writer, both in academia and in the literary world. She has several works to her credit, key among which is her path-breaking study of Partition, The Other Side of Silence: Voices from the Partition of India, which won the Oral History Book Association Award and the Nikkei Asia Award for Culture. She has also taught publishing for over 20 years.Critical Conversations on Sunday, 19 April 2026These two conversations honour Dalit History Month by focusing on the work of  Dalit writers Anita Bharti and Sumitra Mehrol who will speak onthe intersections of caste and gender and how these plays out in Dalit women’s writing and their lives.15:30 onwardsAnita Bharti in conversation with Shabani HussainiwalaAnita Bhartiis a well-known and widely read Dalit writer who writes fiction, poetry, non-fiction.She has edited several volumes on Dalit women’s writing and has also put together a collection of her own essays on caste and gender. Anita is also an activist who works on the histories and literature of Dalit women.Shabani Hassanwaliais a writer and filmmaker, making non-fiction text, sound and images in various media since the year 2000. Her work engages with changing socio-political realities, volatile subcultures and intimate personal histories in an India-in-transition. A graduate in English Literature, Delhi University and post graduate from Mass Communication Research Centre, Jamia Millia Islamia, her feature documentaries includeBeing Bhaijaan(2014),Gali(2017) andOut of Thin Air(2009). She is an INLAKS Fellow, and worked at The Sundance Institute, Los Angeles and the Documentary Filmmakers Group, London, as part of the fellowship.18:00 onwardsSumitra Mehrol in conversation and Rita KothariSumitra Mehrolis a writer, and a teacher at Delhi University. Her autobiography (in Hindi)TootePankhon se ParwaazTak, tells the story of being disabled and dalit and speaks of the multiple discriminations she faced in her life and how she overcame them.Rita Kothariis professor of English at Ashoka University. An accomplished writer, multilingual scholar and translator, she is also co-director of the Ashoka Centre for Translation. She has translated extensively from Gujarati and Sindhi into English, and vice versa. She has a body of important work on the Sindhi experience of Partition, particularly her bookThe Burden of Refuge: Partition Experiences of the Sindhis of Gujarat(2009). Her most recent book,Uneasy Translations: Self, Experience and Indian Literature(2022), interweaves her personal journey as an academic into reflections around self, language, and translation.Scan the code for details:Please note:Parking is not available at the Goethe-Institut. You may find parking at the NDMC parking near the Ambadeep Building. Alternatively, you can come to the Goethe-Institut via taxi or auto-rickshaw.

name: Toubab | Directed by Florian Dietrich

location.name: Goethe-Institut / Max Mueller Bhavan New  New

description: Tuesday, 5 May 2026, 19:00Freshly released from prison, Babtou is handcuffed again by law enforcement officers on the very evening of his welcome party, where everything goes thoroughly wrong. This time, however, the consequences could hardly be more drastic, because although he was born in Germany and his home is Frankfurt, Babtou is to be deported to Senegal. He only knows Senegal from his father's stories, and so Babtou and his buddy Dennis want to prevent the deportation at all costs - which only seems possible by entering into a marriage. As a macho man, however, Babtou has bad cards with the women in the neighborhood, so Dennis soon has to serve as a spouse instead. The word toubab, to which the name Babtou alludes, is a common term used for white Europeans in Central and West Africa.
